coredata.dc:title: N-doped and N, Si-doped carbon dots for enhanced corrosion inhibition of mild steel in acidic environment
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmed, Gaber Hashem Gaber
El-Naggar, G.A.
Nasr, F.
Matter, E.A.
coredata.dc:description: Two types of carbon dots (C-Dots), N-doped and N, Si-doped, were synthesized through a hydrothermal technique and named CD1 and CD2, respectively. TEM, SEM, XRD, FTIR, UV–vis spectrophotometric absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) analyses were utilized to characterize the synthesized C-Dots. The potential of CD1 and CD2 to inhibit the corrosion of mild steel (MS) in 1 mol. L−1 HCl was studied electrochemically by potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques along with the complementary surface studies. Both C-Dots were quasi-spherical with nanometer-sized nucleation clusters dispersed in the liquid phase. CD2 nanoparticles showed a significant level of crystallization, while CD1 corresponded to a group of sp2 graphitic carbon with stacking faults. Similar optical properties were obtained for both synthesized C-dots with absorption in the range 200–350 nm, emission at around 420 nm and wavelength-independent emission properties. The maximum inhibition efficiency (IE%) obtained at 298 K and 200 mg L−1 for CD1 and CD2 were 91.90 % and 94.37 %, respectively. The concentration of inhibitors had a direct correlation with IE, as the C-Dots were more readily adsorbed onto the MS surface, creating a protective film. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm gave the best fit compared to the kinetic thermodynamic model. The type of charge on the metal surface and the chemical structure of the inhibitor impact the adhesion of the inhibitor to the surface. Additionally, chemisorption and physisorption played a critical role in the strong interaction between the synthesized C-dots and MS surface. Utilizing synthesized C-Dots is a novel, eco-friendly, and effective strategy to prevent corrosion in MS when exposed to a 1 M HCl solution for an extended period.

Potentiodynamic polarization curves of MS immersed in 1 M HCl in the absence and absence of different concentrations of (a) CD1 and (b) CD2 nanoparticles at 298 K.
The representation of Langmuir, kinetic thermodynamic, Temkin, and Frenudlish isotherms plots of the inhibitors; CD1 and CD2 on mild steel in 1 M HCl at 298 K.

head.ce:title: N-doped and N, Si-doped carbon dots for enhanced corrosion inhibition of mild steel in acidic environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two types of carbon dots (C-Dots), N-doped and N, Si-doped, were synthesized through a hydrothermal technique and named CD1 and CD2, respectively. TEM, SEM, XRD, FTIR, UV–vis spectrophotometric absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) analyses were utilized to characterize the synthesized C-Dots. The potential of CD1 and CD2 to inhibit the corrosion of mild steel (MS) in 1 mol. L−1 HCl was studied electrochemically by potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques along with the complementary surface studies. Both C-Dots were quasi-spherical with nanometer-sized nucleation clusters dispersed in the liquid phase. CD2 nanoparticles showed a significant level of crystallization, while CD1 corresponded to a group of sp2 graphitic carbon with stacking faults. Similar optical properties were obtained for both synthesized C-dots with absorption in the range 200–350 nm, emission at around 420 nm and wavelength-independent emission properties. The maximum inhibition efficiency (IE%) obtained at 298 K and 200 mg L−1 for CD1 and CD2 were 91.90 % and 94.37 %, respectively. The concentration of inhibitors had a direct correlation with IE, as the C-Dots were more readily adsorbed onto the MS surface, creating a protective film. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm gave the best fit compared to the kinetic thermodynamic model. The type of charge on the metal surface and the chemical structure of the inhibitor impact the adhesion of the inhibitor to the surface. Additionally, chemisorption and physisorption played a critical role in the strong interaction between the synthesized C-dots and MS surface. Utilizing synthesized C-Dots is a novel, eco-friendly, and effective strategy to prevent corrosion in MS when exposed to a 1 M HCl solution for an extended period.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscience and nanotechnology involve the manipulation of materials at the atomic or molecular level. In recent years, nanotechnology has gained popularity across various academic disciplines, such as engineering, physics, chemistry, biology, biomedical engineering, and space technology. In nanotechnology, the term “particles” refers to small objects that behave as complete units in terms of their characteristics and transport phenomena. Nanomaterials are essential and a subject of intense scientific study because they connect certain atoms and molecules to the appropriate bulk materials [1].

Over the past two decades, carbon-based nanomaterials have been the focus of much research interest due to their unique photoelectronic and physicochemical properties compared to their bulk structures. Carbon nanodots are the most common types of carbon nanomaterials. Carbon dots (C-Dots) were named after their main formative element (C) and have rapidly developed in various fields of science. C-Dots offer notable features over many similar nanoparticles such as good aqueous solubility, ease, and diversity of synthetic approaches at low costs, low cytotoxicity, robust chemical inertness, superior biocompatibility, good optical properties of strong optical absorption and bright fluorescence, high fluorescence emission quantum yields and excellent resistance to photobleaching and photoblinking. Such properties make them highly promising for potential applications in diverse fields of science, including sensing, imaging, targeting, catalysis, protection, and photovoltaic devices, among others [2,3].

The exceptional strength and durability of steel make it a preferred material for a wide range of applications, including construction, infrastructure, transportation, and manufacturing. It is commonly used in equipment, ships, trains, vehicles, machinery, and household appliances. However, despite its many advantages, mild steel is highly susceptible to significant corrosion when subjected to acid-based cleaning procedures [4]. As a result, protective mechanisms are necessary for the effective use of metallic materials in technical and commercial applications. In some metal/environment systems, the metal is shielded by passivity. In contrast, the metal surface is still active in other cases, necessitating the provision of some type of protection either through conditioning of the metal or the environment, or by using electrochemical control [5]. The use of chemical corrosion inhibitors has become essential to prevent corrosion of metal surfaces in a variety of media, including acidic, alkaline, saline, and other aggressive media, thus prolonging the life of the metal while having minimal impact on the metal's characteristics [6].

The corrosion inhibitor can either slow down the anodic process and/or the cathodic process by simply blocking the active sites on the metal surface. The metal may also enter the passivation area, where a natural oxide coating forms, and the corrosion may be inhibited by substances known as passivation inhibitors, such as NO2 −, CrO4 2−, Pb3O4, and Ca2PbO4. Additionally, some inhibitors, such as phosphates, silicates, hydroxides, and bicarbonates, work by contributing to the production of a thin coating on the surface of the metal, which inhibits the corrosion process. Chemical, physical, or combined adsorption inhibitors are made of chemical structures with -NH2, -OH−, -COO−, etc. [7].

Unfortunately, some of the most efficient organic corrosion inhibitors, such as benzothiazole and thiourea derivatives, are toxic, hardly biodegradable, and hazardous to the environment. As a result, there has been an increase in large-scale initiatives to synthesize more eco-friendly alternatives with fewer negative effects on the ecosystem. One such novel and environmentally beneficial inhibitor is C-Dots, which requires further research on its corrosion inhibition properties for various types of metals and alloys. In this study, we utilized surface modification technology with C-Dots nanoparticles as an easy and cost-effective approach to synthesize two distinct structures of C-Dots (N and N, Si doped C-Dots). We investigated their efficiency as corrosion inhibitors for MS in a 1 M HCl solution using EIS, PDP, and surface studies. Additionally, we examined the utility of the synthesized C-Dots in corrosion research and evaluated their inhibition efficiency using relevant electrochemical techniques.

Chitosan from ACRŌS ORGANICS (http://www.acros.com), 8-hydroxyquinoline (extra pure) 99 % from Loba Chemie company, di-Ammonium hydrogen citrate from El Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals company, (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (3-APTES) ≥ 98.0 % from Sigma-Aldrich (http://www.sigmaaldrich.com), ammonia solution (∼ 30–33 %) from chemajet chemical company, glacial acetic acid, and HCl 32 % from El-Gomhouria Chemicals company (https://el-gomhouria.com/en/), and cerium (III) chloride.7aq 98.5 % from Chem-Lab NV were all purchased for use in both the preparation and application processes. Distilled H2O was used throughout the preparations and measurements. All commercial reagents were used without further purification.

The N-doped C-Dots (CD1) were synthesized by carbonizing ammonium citrate dibasic with ammonia (Scheme 1 ) using a similar method as reported by Li et al. [8]. Initially, 2 g of ammonium citrate was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. This solution was then placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave chamber along with 2 mL of a 25 % ammonia aqueous solution and maintained at 180 °C for 6 h in an oven for further reaction. The autoclave was then allowed to cool down to room temperature, resulting in a dark-brown solution with dark-brown colloidal particles. To remove large carbon clusters and leftover compounds, the resulting solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm. The treated solution was then filtered through a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.45 μm and subjected to ultra-purification via dialysis tubes (MWCO = 3.5 KDa) against water for 3 days to yield an aqueous solution of CD1. The purified N-doped C-Dots were finally obtained and dried under vacuum at −50°C. The final product of CD1 was stored at 3 °C until further characterization and application.

The modified approach outlined by Keerthana et al. [9] was used to synthesize N,Si-doped C-Dots (CD2), as illustrated in Scheme 1. Typically, 1 g of chitosan precursor and 40 mL of glacial acetic acid were ultrasonically mixed until all solids dissolved. Then, 4 mL of 3-APTES was added as the Si-dopant. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and the reaction was allowed to take place in an oven at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling spontaneously to room temperature in ambient settings, a dark brown solution was produced. The solution was filtered through a three-layered Whatman (No. 32.0) filter paper, followed by cooling centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 20 min to detach large aggregates. Finally, dark brown gel particles were produced after the obtained C-Dots were maintained at −80 °C for a short time and then moved to vacuum drying at −50 °C for 24 h.

A variety of characterization approaches were utilized to explore the new physical, chemical, optical, and mechanical properties of nanoparticles, taking advantage of their prospective uses. To achieve ultrahigh resolution and imaging of nano-scaled materials, the JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) was employed at an acceleration voltage of 200 Kv. The high-performance JSM-6400 scanning electron microscope from JEOL Ltd. (Japan) was used to take SEM micrographs of C-Dots. The FTIR spectra of the C-Dots samples were recorded using a German FTIR spectrometer (TENSOR 27, Bruker). The structural analysis, crystallinity, and purity of C-Dots were carried out using a GNR-X-Ray Diffractometer (Model: APD 2000 PRO, USA) with CuK radiation (=1.50598) at 35 mA and 35 kV in 0.03° steps/scan with an integration time of one s/step. The absorption spectra of highly diluted aqueous solutions of C-Dots samples were evaluated using the high-performance T80 Double Beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer from PG Instruments Ltd., with a wavelength range of 190–1100 nm. A Fluorolog (Horiba Jobin-Yvon) spectrometer was utilized for the photoluminescence analysis of C-Dots, with a slit width of 5 nm for both excitation and emission.

Commercially purchased mild steel reinforcing rod with the chemical composition listed in Table 1 was used in all corrosion inhibition studies. The chemical composition was determined by FOUNDRY-MASTER Optical Emission Spectrometer (OES), Model/Hitachi High-Tech, America. The steel rods' surfaces were polished using progressively finer grades of SiC sandpaper, ranging from 160 to 1500 grit. This resulted in a surface that was flat and had a mirror sheen. The rod was then treated with ethanol to eliminate any remaining impurities, cleaned with distilled water, dried, and used without further storage. The experimental corrosive electrolytes used in all tests were prepared by diluting analytical grade 32 % HCl with distilled water to produce a 1 M HCl solution. The inhibitors (CD1 or CD2) were tested at different concentrations of 200, 100, 50, and 25 mg L−1 to determine the optimal concentration that produced the greatest MS inhibition efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurement system and electrochemical cell
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we used the PGSTAT128N by Metrohm Autolab, a 12 V/800 mA potentiostat/galvanostat electrochemical cell analyzer from the Autolab N series (Serial no. AUT86580, Manufactured by Metrohm Autolab B.V., Utrecht, The Netherlands). The device was powered by a computer running Nova 1.11 software and was utilized for conducting EIS and PDP measurements.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out in a classical three-electrode Pyrex glass cell with a capacity of 100 mL, at temperatures ranging from 298 to 328 K. The reference and working electrodes were Ag/AgCl and MS electrodes, respectively, with an exposed surface area of 0.385 cm2. A platinum counter electrode (Pt) was used.

To ensure consistency, all experiments were conducted at constant temperatures (within ±1 °C), with the aid of a water bath to adjust the temperature. Prior to beginning the measurements (PDP & EIS), we performed an open-circuit potential (OCP) test for 30 min to achieve a steady potential.

During EIS measurements, an alternating perturbation with an amplitude signal of ±5 mV was applied within the frequency range 104 - 10−2 Hz at Eocp. For PDP measurements, we moved the working electrode from −300 to +300 mV of the Eocp, with a scan rate of 5 mVs−1, immediately after EIS.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy was used to study the surface morphology of the corroded and inhibited mild steel surfaces. The instrument used was the FE-SEM/EDAX-QUANTA FEG 250 (Netherlands), which has an accelerating voltage of 20 kV and is equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy detector. Additionally, the inhibited solution containing 200 mg L−1 C-Dots at 298 K was subjected to UV–Vis absorbance examination before and after the MS was submerged for 24 h using a T80 Double Beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

Fig. 1 displays the microscopic results of CD1 and CD2 nanoparticles in diluted aqueous solutions with high resolution. The sizes and crystal morphologies of the synthesized C-Dots vary, and thus, the composition of the precursors and the synthesis conditions have a significant impact on the structural characteristics of C-Dots.

According to SEM images, CD1 nanoparticles showed high-density growth and quasi-spherical shapes (micrograph 1a) [10]. On the other hand, micrograph 1b shows CD2 as well-distributed, quasi-spherical, and nanosized particles [11].

The TEM results of the C-Dots are compared in micrographs 1c and 1d. As shown in micrograph 1c, CD1 nanoparticles continued to have oval shapes and varied diameters between 4.07 and 10.9 nm. Micrograph 1d shows that the quasi-spherical CD2 nanoparticles are monodispersed and narrowly distributed in size, ranging from 3.57 to 4.81 nm [12,13].

The morphological data support quasi-spherical morphologies ranging in nanometer size from 1 to 10 nm, which also indicate the presence of numerous surface defects bound to the C-Dots' surfaces and enhance the solubility of C-Dots in water. However, when diffused in water, CD1 nanoparticles are more likely to form agglomerates at high concentrations.

X-ray diffraction measurements were used to investigate the structural analysis, crystallinity, and purity of the C-Dots. The XRD pattern of CD1 within the 2θ range of 10–80° showed a broad peak at 2θ = 20.15°, which was attributed to the (002) lattice spacing of CD1 with a turbostratic disorder [14]. This observation supports the interlayer spacing (d) of CD1 at 0.440 nm, which confirms the weak crystalline nature of carbon dots and indicates its amorphous nature. The characteristic peak of CD2, shown in Fig. 2b at ∼2θ = 23.51°, corresponds to graphite's 002 crystallographic planes, with an interplanar spacing of 0.378 nm. This spacing is extremely close to the interplanar spacing in graphite (0.34 nm) and demonstrates the dominance of the highly conjugated sp2 domains in the cores of CD2 nanoparticles [13,15]. Furthermore, all XRD patterns exclusively displayed the distinctive peak of carbon dots at 2θ = 20–30°, providing evidence of the purity of C-Dots.

The FTIR results shown in Fig. 2c-d and summarized in Table 1S of the Electronic Supplementary Materials (ESM) reveal the evolution of graphene/graphite-like structures and edges formed with –COOH, –CONR, –OH, –NH2, etc. [16–18]. These findings confirm the successful amidation process of CD1. Moreover, the typical absorption of cumulated double bonds (N=C=O and C=C=N) is unique to N-CDs and distinguishes them from their precursor analogs.

The FTIR results of CD2 (Fig. 2d) indicate the successful covalent conjugation between 3–APTES and chitosan through amide bonds, as evidenced by the strong peak at ∼1633.1 cm−1 [19]. The stretching vibration of the CC of benzenoid ring is compatible with the absorption maxima at ∼1560 and 765 cm−1, suggesting intramolecular dehydration and the presence of the main component of graphene/graphite. This result confirms the successful transformation of the precursors to C-Dots. The structures of silane (SiH) at 2110.3 cm−1 and organic siloxane/silicone (Si–O–C) at 1124 cm−1 indicate that chitosan and a silane coupling agent successfully formed a covalent bond, and the Si atoms were successfully doped in the C-dots structure [16].

Based on the FTIR measurements, it can be concluded that both Si and N atoms were successfully doped into the CD2 structure, whereas N atoms were successfully doped into the CD1 structure. Nitrogen atoms may be found in the core of the C-Dots nanoparticles as aromatic structures, but on the surface as amine and amide groups. These complex nanoscale carbonaceous materials exhibit a high degree of structural unit dispersion, conjugated CC domains, and different functional groups on the surface, which may result in intriguing polar nature and optical properties.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CD1 and CD2 in aqueous nanoparticle solutions are compared in Fig. 1Sa (ESM). CD1 exhibits a high absorption peak at 235 nm and another peak at around 335 nm, while CD2 only displays a low-intensity absorption “shoulder” around 300 nm. The absorption of the C-Dots is primarily due to the surface functionalities of the nanoparticles. In general, the synthesized C-Dots display strong UV absorption bands between 230 and 350 nm [20]. The high-energy UV absorption peak at 235 nm is assigned to the π → π* transitions of the aromatic CC bonds in the carbon core. The low-energy UV absorption peak at 300–330 nm is attributed to n → π* transitions by non-bonding orbitals such as O − H, N − H, C=N/C=O, and/or C − N bonds, or other connected surface functional groups. The tail obtained in the visible region indicates that no further forms of nanocarbon were produced during the partial carbonization, verifying the purity of the formed C-Dots [21]. Figs. 1Sb and 1Sc (ESM) display the PL spectra of the carbon dots. Each of the investigated carbon dots has the greatest PL emission in the blue region of the visible spectrum, with λem at approximately 425 and 430 nm for CD1 and CD2, respectively. Meanwhile, all the carbon dots display excitation-independent photoluminescence, indicating that each type of carbon nanodot appears to have a comparable composition, structure, and luminous centers [22]. CD1 exhibits higher fluorescence intensity than CD2. Although the mechanism of C-dot PL is not fully understood, the development of sp2 aromatic domains and surface defects formed by dehydration, oxidation, and amidation are mostly responsible for the PL mechanism of the examined C-Dots [19].

Fig. 2S (ESM) depicts the variation of mild steel's OCP with exposure time in 1 M HCl, in the absence and presence of various concentrations of C-Dots at 298 K. The OCP values of mild steel in free HCl showed unstable oscillations, indicating a greater susceptibility to corrosion. In contrast, the OCP value in C-Dots solutions initially rose sharply to higher negative values before plateauing. This suggested that the corrosion reaction initially slowed down with time before reaching a quasi-equilibrium rate [23].

The rapid migration rate of C-Dots due to the nanosizes formed more stable, insoluble, and protective films, which resulted in the reduced corrosion rate. The presence of particles with different sizes, shapes, or adsorption orientations caused noticeable variations in the OCP values of C-Dots solutions. The OCP value increased to more noble values with an increase in CD1 concentration in the electrolyte. The more positive potential led to a lower corrosion rate.

The anodic Tafel slope observed for CD1 inhibitor was larger than that observed for MS in free HCl. The kinetic plots for the CD1 inhibitor and the values of the kinetic parameters confirmed this observation. This indicated that the anodic corrosion sites at which CD1 was adsorbing on the MS surface experienced some degree of surface passivation, which increased the MS surface protection against corrosion [24].

The CD2 nanoparticle adsorption on the surface of MS may prevent H+ adsorption and discharge at the cathode region, leading to an increase in the overpotential of the hydrogen evolution process. This increased the OCP values to be more negative, demonstrating that the CD2 inhibitor inhibited the cathodic reaction more effectively than the anodic dissolution [25]. Increasing the concentration of the inhibitor led to the possibility of OCP being shifted more negatively.

We examined the corrosion behavior of MS electrodes in 1 M HCl solution in the presence and absence of all C-Dots at various concentrations using EIS. The Nyquist and Bode graphs were used to display the measurements. We observed that the Nyquist plots for the CD1 inhibitor (Fig. 3a) consisted of a single semicircle at high frequencies and straight lines at low frequencies, indicating charge transfer and diffusion processes. However, the impedance spectra of the CD2 inhibitor (Fig. 3b) showed two semicircles due to the occurrence of the corrosion reaction and the structure of the CD2 adsorbed film [26]. The capacitive loops' diameter consistently increased as the C-Dots concentration in the electrolyte increased, indicating a significant increase in mild steel's resistance to corrosion. Charge transfer across heterogeneous electrodes appears as a common feature in Nyquist plots due to their imperfect semicircular shape. The heterogeneity of the surface can be attributed to the MS composition, the corrosion products, or the heterogeneity of the inhibitor film that is adsorbed on the surface [27].

The Bode modulus graphs demonstrate a strong correlation between the magnitude of impedance modulus and the anti-corrosion properties of materials (|Z|f = 0.01 Hz) [28]. In Fig. 3b and d, it is evident that the |Z|f = 0.01 Hz values are significantly higher with increasing C-Dots concentration, showing an upward trend of approximately 1–2 orders at different inhibitor concentrations. The |Z|f = 0.01 Hz value for the blank solution is 41.59 Ω.cm2. However, at 200 mg L−1 of CD1 and CD2 solutions, it increases to 500.23 and 587.46 Ω.cm2, respectively, indicating a decrease in the corrosion rate of the MS electrodes. Fig. 3b shows a unique time constant in the middle frequency range of the Bode-phase spectra, which is related to charge transfer at the metal/electrolyte interface. For MS/CD2, two constants are observed (Fig. 3d), as the contribution of the oxide layer is considered [26,29]. Additionally, the phase shifts of the sinusoidal current to the potential are <90°, indicating non-ideal capacitor behavior. Compared to uninhibited samples, the size of the loop widens for the inhibited sample, increasing as inhibitor concentration rises. The findings suggest that all inhibitors exhibit interfacial inhibition behavior, becoming more capacitive as the concentration increases [30,31].

To quantitatively evaluate and interpret impedance spectra, we performed structural impedance modeling. We modeled the spectra recorded for MS in 1 M HCl or containing different concentrations of CD1 using the classic Randle's cell, [R(Q[RW])], with an additional Warburg element (Fig. 3e). This provides evidence of the presence of diffusion processes [32]. For CD2, a better fit was obtained using an equivalent circuit with two RC units [R(Q[R(QR)])] (Fig. 5f). The second RC unit in the second circuit was related to the inhibitor film formed on the metal surface after exposure to the electrolyte. Despite its relatively low resistance and minimal protective ability, the film had an impact on the form of the impedance spectra. Fig. 3g represents a fitting example of Nyquist diagrams for MS in 1 M HCl in the absence and presence of a 200 mg L−1 inhibitor. The comparison between the experimental and simulated Nyquist plots showed excellent agreement. Furthermore, the models accurately predicted the impedance values based on concentration. In addition, the EIS experimental results were fitted to an equivalent circuit, which yielded all the impedance parameters. The summary of these parameters is presented in Tables 2 and 3
.

To approximate the values of the respective capacitances of the double electric layer and film layer, we used constant phase elements (Qdl and Qf) instead of ideal capacitors (Cdl and Cf). It is important to note that the impedance function of the CPE, as well as that of the pure capacitors, is a frequency-dependent characteristic. The equation for these impedance functions is provided below [27,31,33]:

), and (ω) is the angular frequency. The parameter (n) represents surface heterogeneity and is a valuable criterion for determining the nature of the metal surface, particularly in the case of CPE. If (n) equals −1, 0, 0.5, or 1, CPE represents either an inductor, resistor, Warburg impedance, or pure capacitor, respectively. The double-layer capacitance (Cdl) [27] and relaxation time constant (τ) of a surface state can be computed using the following equations:

Tables 4 and 5
present the values of the corrosion process parameters obtained by fitting the spectra to the equivalent circuit, and η EIS% was calculated using Eq. (4) [28].

Where

are the polarization resistance in the inhibited and uninhibited solutions, respectively.

The impedance modeling provided numerical values that indicated the corrosion process at the interface between MS and the corrosive medium containing C-Dots. The following observations can be made:
1.
The values of Rf and Rct increased with the addition of C-Dots. The creation of the protective coating at the MS/electrolyte interface was indicated by the further enhancement of these values with increasing C-Dots concentration.
2.
The CPEdl and CPEf values were attributed to the capacitive behavior of the electrochemical double layer and the adsorbed C-Dots film. These values exhibited an increasing trend with an increase in C-Dots concentration. It is noteworthy that the capacitance of a two-plate condenser can be used to approximate the capacitance of an electrochemical double layer, as described in [27]:
(5)
C dl
=
ɛ o
ɛ
A / d

Here, ɛo represents the permittivity of the atmosphere, ɛ is the dielectric constant of the medium, A denotes the surface of the electrode, and d represents the thickness of the protective layer. As the concentration of C-Dots increases and more H2O molecules are replaced, the protective layer becomes thicker. As a result, the lower trend of CPEdl with increasing concentration in the solution may be viewed as “anticipated” because it leads to a decrease in the dielectric constant of the electrochemical double layer (d) [27,34,35].
3.
The heterogeneity parameter values ranged from 0.75 to 1, indicating a non-ideal capacitive response of the CPE for all fitted experimental impedance data.
4.
The appearance of the Warburg impedance element was attributed to the electrochemical reactions at MS/electrolyte (blank or CD1) interface under diffusion control.
5.
The values of τ in the presence of the inhibitors, for instance, τ = 0.0149 and 0.0215 s at 200 mg L−1 CD1 and CD2, respectively were slightly higher than that of the blank (0.0061 s), demonstrating the spontaneous adsorption process of C-Dots [31].
6.
The data showed that as the concentration of C-Dots increased, the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η EIS %) also increased. At 200 mg L−1 of CD1 and CD2, the maximum values of 92.89 % and 94.09 %, respectively, were reached, indicating strong surface corrosion protection by C-Dots nanoparticles. Furthermore, the estimated trend, along with the trend in CPE values, suggested that the corrosion protection efficiency depended on the amount of C-Dots nanoparticles covering the surface to create the protective layer [28,32,35,36]. Table 4 presents a comparison of the inhibition efficiency of the C-Dots synthesized here with other recently published inhibitors for the corrosion inhibition of MS in acidic environments. Notably, our C-Dots exhibit several advantages over other inhibitors, including their ease of synthesis, environmentally friendly nature, and high efficiency.
7.
The C-Dots' inhibitory effects could be explained by the following relationship: CD2 > CD1. Notably, the CD2 solutions of the investigated inhibitors displayed the highest Rp and |Z|
f = 0.01 Hz values and the lowest Cdl values, and thus provided the highest inhibition. The fitting information and the experimental finding were in good agreement, as indicated by the chi-square (χ2) parameter in the order of 10−2.

PDP measurements were carried out for MS in a 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of various concentrations of C-Dots. The results were used to determine the mechanism of action of the C-Dots, i.e., whether they functioned as anodic, cathodic, or mixed inhibitors, and to confirm the information obtained from EIS measurements. According to Fig. 4a and b, CD1 and CD2 acted as anodic and cathodic inhibitors for the partial anodic and cathodic reactions. The anodic and cathodic Tafel lines shifted to lower current densities with increasing concentration of C-Dots. The quasi-parallel cathodic and anodic potentiodynamic polarization branches indicated that the adsorption of all investigated C-Dots affected the kinetics of corrosion reactions rather than changing the corrosion mechanism itself [43]. This result demonstrated that C-Dots nanoparticles adsorbed onto the MS surface, and the extent of adsorption was related to the C-Dots concentration, suggesting the formation of a film [24]. Table 5 shows the extrapolated polarization parameters, including corrosion current density (i
corr ), corrosion potential (E
corr ), slopes of the anodic and cathodic Tafel curves (βa and -βc), polarization resistances (R p), and corrosion rates (CR). The Tafel method was used to calculate the corrosion inhibition efficiency according to Eq. (6) [44].
(6)
η
%
=
i °
corr
−
i corr
i °
corr
× 100 %
Where
i °
corr
and
i corr
denote the corrosion current densities of the uninhibited and inhibited steel, respectively.

The overall evaluation of the PDP data is as follows:
1.
The i
corr values and the corrosion rate of the MS continually decrease with an increase in the concentration of C-Dots. As investigated, the CR of MS was very rapid and scored 20.6 mm/year in 1 M HCl medium but dropped sharply to 1.66 and 1.15 mm/year at 200 mg L−1 CD1 and CD2, respectively. In comparison with the i
corr value of 1761.2 mA/cm2 in the uninhibited medium, smaller values of 142.69 and 99.15 mA/cm2 were observed in the presence of 200 mg L−1 of C-Dots, respectively.
2.
The C-Dots are adsorbed on the MS surface, leading to intensive surface coverage and hence enhanced IE. The corrosion inhibition efficiency first increased rapidly, then gradually approached, and yielded a maximum protection efficiency of 91.90 % and 94.37 % at 200 mg L−1 of CD1 and CD2, respectively. The results confirmed that the synthesized C-Dots were excellent anti-polarizing agents for MS in 1 M HCl solution [45,46].
3.
The similarity of the anodic and cathodic slopes across all C-Dots concentrations suggests that the addition of C-Dots into the electrolyte did not influence the mechanisms of MS corrosion reactions. E
corr is displaced to values no higher than ±85 mV at 200 mg L−1 of either CD1 or CD2 inhibitors, confirming the mixed nature of all C-Dots when they interacted with the MS surface. Additionally, the C-Dots inhibited the corrosion reaction mainly based on the surface blocking mechanism [47].
4.
The presence of -NH2, -COOH, N-C=O, OCH3, and other groups in the molecules could be responsible for the efficacy of the adsorption of C-Dots on MS surface in the acidic medium, which caused the blockage of active sites.
5.
The data yielded by three different techniques are remarkably consistent. Additionally, it validates the data accuracy and the approaches used to treat the data. As a result, all synthesized C-Dots can serve as effective MS corrosion inhibitors in applications involving acidic electrolytes.

The remarkable inhibitory effect of C-Dots on MS surface corrosion could be attributed to their adsorption behavior. Therefore, adsorption isotherms were crucial in complementing the study of the inhibitory mechanism of C-Dots. Fig. 5 shows Langmuir, Kinetic Thermodynamic, Temkin, and Freundlich isotherms [48] used to fit the potentiodynamic experimental results of MS corrosion in 1 M HCl in the presence of CD1 and CD2. The adsorption parameters are presented in Table 6 .

Fig. 5 demonstrates good agreement for the adsorption of C-Dots on the surface of MS, according to both the Langmuir and kinetic-thermodynamic isotherms. Comparing the R2 values, Langmuir provides the best fit. All studied C-Dots molecules adsorb onto the MS surface according to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm (R2 ≅ 1), indicating one-layer adsorption of the inhibitors on the MS with negligible interactions between adsorbed particles [49]. The kinetic-thermodynamic model shows that the inverse of the slopes (1/y) of the obtained straight lines is not much larger than unity, confirming that each active site can adsorb only a molecule of C-Dots. The K ads values derived from the intercepts of Langmuir plots were used to calculate the standard free energy of adsorption
ΔG °
ads
using Eq. (9):
(9)
ΔG °
ads
= − RT
ln
1000 ×
K ads

R (8.314 J/mol.K) represents the universal gas constant, T (K) represents the absolute temperature, and the 1000 value (g L−1) represents the mass concentration of water in solution, since the unit of K ads was reported in terms of L/g.
ΔG °
ads
(J/mol) represents the free energy of adsorption.

Table 7 presents the values of K
ads and
ΔG °
ads
for all C-Dots inhibitors. The constant values (K ads) are notably high, and they follow the sequence of CD2 > CD1, indicating the strong adsorption capacity of all C-Dots on the MS surface. The
ΔG °
ads
values range from −20 kJ/mol to −40 kJ/mol, which confirms a mixed type of chemical and physical adsorption mechanisms. However, the adsorption mechanism of CD1 may be attributed to the electrostatic interaction between the functional groups of the inhibitor, mainly the electron-rich –COOH, –NH2 and N–C=O groups, and the MS surface. The high negative Gibbs free energy of adsorption values for all investigated C-Dots indicate that the adsorption process is highly spontaneous [49].

The effect of temperature on the inhibition of MS corrosion in the presence and absence of 200 mg L−1 C-Dots in 1 M HCl was determined using PDP measurements. The measurements were conducted at various temperatures ranging from 298 K to 328 K with a 10 K interval. The results are presented in Fig. 6 , while the extrapolated data is listed in Table 2S (ESM). The findings showed that an increase in temperature led to an increase in corrosion reaction kinetics, regardless of the presence of C-Dots inhibitor in the electrolyte. This was due to an increased corrosion rate caused by the inhibitor layer's desorption or degradation at high temperatures, leading to an increased exposure of the metal surface to the acidic corrosive solution. All produced C-Dots showed a drop in η% with an increase in temperature, indicating weak Vander Waals forces responsible for physical adsorption, which tended to vanish at high temperatures [50]. However, by comparing the R p values at a fixed temperature, it was evident that all synthesized C-Dots acted as effective corrosion inhibitors throughout the entire temperature range.

In the field of corrosion studies, the dependence of the i
corr on temperature is considered an Arrhenius-type process, and its rate is given by [30]:
(10)
i corr
= A
exp
−
E a
RT

The symbol A represents the Arrhenius frequency factor, which varies with the metal type and electrolyte. E
a is the activation energy of the corrosion process. Fig. 3Sa shows the plot of Ln i
corr as a function of the inverse of the absolute temperature (1000/T) for MS electrodes in the absence and presence of 200 mg L−1 of CD1 or CD2 inhibitors.

Arrhenius plots yielded a straight line with a slope of (−E
a
/R). This allowed us to calculate the values of the activation energies E
a (included in Table 8 ). Additionally, we verified the temperature effect by determining the changes in activation enthalpy ΔH* and activation entropy ΔS* using the transition state equation as follows [51]:
(11)

Where N and h represent Avogadro's number (6.023 × 1023 mol−1) and Planck's constant (6.626 × 10−34 m2.kg/s), respectively. The transition state plots of Ln i

) of the straight lines obtained and are presented in Table 8.

The following observations support the good performance of C-Dots molecules at higher temperatures:
1.
The values of activation energy obtained in the presence of C-Dots were higher than those obtained in the absence of C-Dots inhibitors, and the values were significantly lower than ±80 kJ/mol. These observations indicate that mixed-type adsorption played a role in inhibiting corrosion reactions. Higher E
a values suggest an increase in the double layer thickness, which improves the activation energy of the corrosion process by increasing the energy barrier for corrosion activity.
2.
The ∆H* values were positive and showed an interesting increase in the presence of C-Dots, indicating an endothermic adsorption of C-Dots on the MS surface. As a result, the dissolution of iron was slowed down.
3.
The E
a values were found to be higher than those of the corresponding enthalpy (ΔH*) analogs, indicating the involvement of a gaseous reaction (the hydrogen evolution reaction) in the corrosion process [52].
4.
The difference between the values of E
a and ΔH* is approximately equal to the average RT value (2.477 kJ/mol) in the studied solutions. This suggests that both corrosion and inhibition processes involve unimolecular interactions.
5.
The values of ΔS* were negative, and in the presence of C-Dots, they were observably less negative, indicating an increase in disorderliness as the reaction progressed from reactants to activated complex. The disorder further increased when C-Dots adsorbed onto the MS surface, because of water molecules desorption. [53].

After being submerged in 1 M HCl with 200 mg L−1 CD1 and CD2 for 48 h, the values of |Z|
f = 0.01 Hz for the MS electrode increased from 500.23 and 587.46. Ω.cm2 to 1526.24 and 1108.69. Ω.cm2, respectively. Additionally, as the immersion time increased, the maximum phase angles also increased. These results suggest a slower corroding system or enhanced protection provided by the C-Dots during long-term immersion.

The EIS data of the steel in the tested solutions that contained CD2 were fitted with the circuit shown in Fig. 3e. Meanwhile, the EIS data of MS in the blank and CD1 solutions were fitted with the circuit in Fig. 7g, which has a one-time constant and an arrangement of [R(QR)].

Table 3S shows the variation of electrochemical parameters of MS in 1 M HCl over time in both the absence and presence of each of the investigated C-Dots. The values of Rct and Cdl depended on the packing of the monolayer of the inhibiting compound. A more densely packed inhibitor monolayer resulted in a higher diameter of the semi-circle, which in turn led to higher Rct and lower Cdl values. With the passage of time, the values of Cdl increased, while the values of Rct decreased in the case of MS/1 M HCl alone, indicating serious corrosion and the formation of more corrosion products. However, the increase in the values of Rct for the inhibited solutions was attributed to the higher surface coverage and the formation of a more compact protective film of C-Dots at the MS/electrolyte interface. The variation in the values of Rct and Cdl for all C-Dots with time followed this order: CD2 < CD1, suggesting that CD1 had the maximum protective effect against HCl corrosion of MS at long immersion times.

The effectiveness of 200 mg L−1 C-Dots inhibitors in inhibiting the corrosion of MS in 1 M HCl was examined using the PDP technique. The acquired values are presented in Table 9 , and Figs. 4Sa and 4Sb depict the results graphically. Fig. 4Sa shows that the corrosion rate of MS/1 M HCl increases rapidly over time. However, the inclusion of C-Dots inhibitors produces the opposite effect, indicating higher corrosion inhibition efficacy. Fig. 4Sb further demonstrates that corrosion inhibition efficiency increases with longer immersion times. With an additional increase in immersion duration, the surface concentration, coverage, and order of the C-Dots monolayer increase, providing a tighter hydrophobic coating for the penetration of aggressive ions to the underlying surface and, therefore, higher corrosion inhibition efficiency.

The synergetic effect occurs when the combined action of two or more factors is superior to the individual effect caused by any of them. In this study, MS samples were exposed to solutions of 1 M HCL containing 200 mg L−1 of either cerium chloride (III), 8-hydroxyquinoline, C-Dots, or combinations of these for 0.5 h before electrochemical measurements were taken. The results of the polarization measurements are depicted in Fig. 5S (ESM), which shows that the mixture of C-Dots and Ce (III) had the highest values of current densities. The current densities decreased with the mixture of C-Dots and 8-HQ, reaching the minimum current density for the mixture of Ce (III), C-Dots, and 8-HQ. Fig. 6S provides a clear illustration of these phenomena, where the impedance spectra were recorded in the form of Nyquist and Bode plots.

The graphs demonstrate that the spectra with the mixture of C-Dots, Ce (III), and 8-HQ had the largest diameter of the capacitive semicircle and the highest value of the impedance modulus (|Z|f = 0.01 Hz), indicating the highest corrosion resistance. The R
p values obtained from the measured polarization curves within a constrained potential range (OCP ±0.030 V) were used to quantitatively evaluate the synergistic impact. Table 4S (ESM) displays the experimentally collected results. From the table, it is evident that the corrosion rates of MS in the presence of C-Dots in combination with Ce (III) are further reduced compared to C-Dots alone. A synergistic effect can be observed in the fact that mixtures of (C-Dots +8-HQ) or (C-Dots + Ce (III) + 8-HQ) raised the IE more than C-Dots alone. However, the synergistic effect of the inhibitors at the mentioned concentration ratios did not produce appreciable degrees of inhibition; rather, the use of C-Dots alone would be more beneficial.

Fig. 7S, Figs. 8, and 9
depict SEM micrographs of the surface of MS samples at low and high magnifications after 6 h of exposure to corrosive media without and with 200 mg L−1 of CD1 or CD2 inhibitors. EDX analysis was performed on the full area of micrographs (a) in SEM analysis to support conclusions drawn from SEM observations.

Micrographs (a) and (b) in Fig. 7S (ESM) reveal pitting corrosion in the absence of inhibitors. The MS exposed to the corrosive medium suffered severe corrosion, resulting in the formation of pores and an oxide layer. The EDX spectrum in Fig. 7S (c) shows a notable appearance of oxygen and chloride, and low iron intensity, indicating significant corrosion of steel.

Fig. 8 shows SEM images of MS exposed to a 1 M HCl solution containing 200 mg L−1 CD1 at low and high magnifications. Micrograph a reveals the presence of grayish-white deposits, whereas micrograph b depicts large agglomerates made up of smaller aggregates at higher magnification. This observation may be attributed to the low stability of CD1 nanoparticles when dispersed in water. Additionally, the EDX-diagram (Fig. 8c) indicates higher Fe, C, and N contents than those of MS/1 M HCl. Therefore, it can be concluded that CD1 caused a certain degree of surface coverage.

The SEM micrograph (a) in Fig. 9 of CD2 reveals a smooth surface with minor pitting formations. Micrograph (b) shows that the surface was enhanced by adsorbed nanodots of CD2. Furthermore, the appearance of Si and N peaks in the EDX analysis can be attributed to the N, Si-doped CD2 inhibitor.

Quantitative data analysis by EDX is illustrated in Fig. 10 . The results of the EDX analysis, along with SEM observations, indicate that CD2 exhibits higher inhibition efficiency than CD1. This comparison reveals variations in the composition, characteristics, and behavior of the C-Dots, which aligns with earlier findings from electrochemical polarization and impedance measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion mechanism and protective anti-corrosion mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrode surface submerged in free hydrochloric acid was found to be readily corroded in surface studies and electrochemical experiments. Due to the flow of electrons generated during oxidation through steel to the cathode, chloride ions increased as per [54]:
(12)
Fe +
3Cl −
↔
FeCl 3
+ 3 e −
(13)
Fe
3 +
+ 3OH − → Fe
OH
3
+ 3Cl −

These interactions caused the electrolyte surrounding the pit area to become negatively charged, whereas the electrolyte inside the pit area became positively charged. The resulting positive charge attracts negative chloride ions, making the pitting process autocatalytic once initiated. This phenomenon was confirmed by SEM observation (Fig. 7S), which showed the formation of large-scale corrosion pits.

The results of the EIS, PDP, and SEM study suggest that the inhibition mechanism of C-Dots is based on adsorption and subsequent formation of a hydrophobic film on the MS surface. This film facilitates the transfer of dissolved corrosive ions from the bulk electrolyte to the MS surface. Langmuir adsorption isotherm and potentiodynamic analysis revealed that all studied C-Dots exhibited a mixed adsorption mechanism on MS through simple coordination and/or electrostatic attraction. Fig. 11 depicts an illustration of the suggested corrosion and inhibition mechanism.

The adhesion of the inhibitor to the surface is impacted by the charge on the metal surface and the chemical structure of the inhibitor. Specifically, in the presence of Cl− ions in an HCl solution, the metal surface is positively charged at the free corrosion potential. The Cl− ions are first adsorbed, resulting in a shift in charge on the solution side of the contact from positive to negative.

In 1 M HCl, the cationic form of C-Dots is dominant, and the protonated form of C-Dots may be electrostatically adsorbed on the MS surface covered with Cl−. The physical adsorption of cations on the MS surface is enhanced by Cl− ions. Additionally, the formation of a coordinate linkage between the unprotonated N and O atoms' lone pair of electrons and the vacant orbital of the Fe atoms may cause chemical adsorption of C-Dots molecules on the steel surface. The presence of cationic species directly adsorbed on the cathodic sites of the MS may also slow down the velocity of the hydrogen evolution reaction [27,34,35].

Electrochemical and surface studies indicate that CD1 nanoparticles have a lower inhibition efficiency than CD2. In Fig. 8, it is observed that the limited stability of CD1 nanoparticles in water results in the formation of agglomerates. These agglomerates cause a decrease in the exposed MS area through physical adsorption and chemical interactions between the functional groups of C-Dots and the steel surface. The decrease in inhibition efficiency due to the desorption of CD1 with an increase in temperature, along with the ∆
G ads °
value, suggests primarily an electrostatic interaction. The physicochemical adsorption of CD2 onto the surface of MS is characterized by a strong interaction at the steel/solution interface, as evidenced by the high Kads value. According to molecular dynamics modeling by Siming Ren et al. [55], the increased inhibitory efficacy of N, Si-doped C-Dots compared to N-doped C-Dots may result from multi-anchor adsorption of N, Si-doped C-Dots on MS surface in strong acidic solutions.

The hydrothermal technique was successfully employed to fabricate N- and N, Si-doped C-Dots, which exhibited quasi-spherical morphologies and a relatively limited particle size distribution in the range of 1–10 nm. FTIR analysis confirmed their graphene/graphite structure, with edges functionalized with various groups such as CO, –OH, –NH2, and/or –NCO, CN, C−N, –COOH, SiH, and Si–O–C, among others. These functional groups contributed to the hydrophilic and polar nature of the synthesized C-Dots and supported their fluorescence, as well as their chemical and optical stability. All the synthesized C-Dots showed strong UV absorption in the range of 220–350 nm, with excitation-independent PL emission at around 420 nm.

The results of the PDP measurements showed that an adsorption-based corrosion inhibition mechanism was compatible with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The corresponding Gibbs energy of adsorption values (−31 kJ.mol−1) were typical for physicochemical adsorption. Although the corrosion inhibition efficiency decreased due to the probable desorption of adsorbed C-Dots molecules, the corrosion inhibition of the MS surface by C-Dots was still efficient at higher temperatures. Thermodynamic calculations of E a and
ΔH ∗
revealed that the adsorption interactions of the C-Dots were spontaneous, endothermic, and unimolecular. Furthermore, the addition of C-Dots decreased the randomness
ΔS ∗
of the corrosion system. Under the tested experimental conditions, all synthesized C-Dots were very effective long-term corrosion inhibitors for MS. Surface morphologies for inhibited and uninhibited MS samples showed clear differences, supporting the development of an adsorbed protective layer on the MS surface in the presence of C-Dots.

The addition of 8-HQ or a combination of Ce (III) and 8-HQ to the solution resulted in a synergistic increase in the IE of C-Dots. However, the addition of Ce (III) to the solution caused a reduction in the IE of C-Dots, possibly due to the different adsorption mechanisms involved.

The multiple adsorption sites present in C-Dots molecules, including -NH2, -COOH, -OH, NCO, and phenyl ring, facilitate adsorption and subsequently block active sites created on the MS surface. This property may be responsible for the corrosion inhibition by C-Dots and its effectiveness in a 1 M HCl solution. Consequently, a monolayer of adsorbed C-Dots forms a protective hydrophobic barrier that prevents corrosive ions from reaching the surface. The multi-anchor adsorption of N, Si-C-Dots on the surface of MS may explain why CD2 has a stronger inhibitory efficacy than CD1.
F. Nasr: Did the experimental work, contribute in explaining data, and write the 1st format of paper.
G. A. El-Naggar: supervision of the experimental work and writing 1st draft.
E. A. Matter: Supervision of experimental work, revision of paper, and explaining data.
Gaber Hashem Gaber Ahmed: Supervision of experimental work, revision of paper, and explaining data.
